posts.name: Nick van der Merwe
posts.cooked:
I turn 40 in November and am thinking it might be fun to open a bottle from that year to celebrate. I have little/no experience with wines of this age unfortunately, and from the research I’ve done online, it looks like options may be very scarce. Budget isn’t an issue up to $1000.

Am I out of luck?

posts.name: Mark  €n+ €r1in€
posts.cooked: Great year for Rioja, could look at a CVNE or Lopez de Heredia Gran Reserva (or both, easily, with that price range). I don’t have much experience with aged Bordeaux but I’ve liked the 1981s that I’ve had, you should also be able to find virtually all classified growths for well under that 1k limit.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
Great year for Rioja, could look at a CVNE or Lopez de Heredia Gran Reserva (or both, easily, with that price range). I don’t have much experience with aged Bordeaux but I’ve liked the 1981s that I’ve had, you should also be able to find virtually all classified growths for well under that 1k limit.

Pomerol did well, particularly Trotanoy. Chateauneuf also.

posts.name: Dale Williams
posts.cooked:
As Mark said very good year in Riojs, I’ve really enjoyed Mugs Prado Enea, LdH (Bosconia GR, Tondonia GR and R, as well as the Tondonia GR white), CVNE Imperial GR. Even cheaper Riojas like Beronia are pretty good.

In Bdx I’ve really liked Trotanoy, Pichon Lalande, and La Mish. You could probably get all three for well under $1000.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: Agree with the above suggestions and would add that 1981 was a strong vintage for classic CA cabernet such as Dunn, Diamond Creek and Ridge, and is one of my favorites. Well stored bottles are well worth seeking out. You can often find large formats, too, which is fun for a party.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
Cellartracker gives some interesting insights (average rating given in the last 5 years):

  1. Krug Vintage 95.9
  2. Heidsieck Cuvee Charlie 94.3
  3. Beaucastel CDP 94.3
  4. DRC La Tache 94.2
  5. Bodegas Contino Reserva 94.2
  6. Unico 94.1
  7. Penfolds Grange 94.1
  8. Laville Haut Brion Blanc 94.0
  9. Lopez de Heredia Tondonia Gran Reserva 93.9
  10. Jordan Cab Sauvignon Alexander Valley 93.6
  11. Yquem 93.3
  12. Lopez de Heredia Bosconia Gran Reserva 93.2
  13. CVNE Vine Real Reserva 93.0
  14. Pichon Baron 93.0
  15. Heitz Marthas 92.8

posts.name: Daniel Bailey
posts.cooked: 81 krug has been great across numerous bottles I’ve tried. If you can find some with good provenance that would be the way I would go.

posts.name: Todd Hamina
posts.cooked: I just read on Instagram that William Kelley is revisiting 81 all year. Maybe follow him and swoop in for some lovelies?

One idea that we have had fun with is using Zodiac years for birthday wines, which also helps for “off” vintages in a particular year. In your case, Year of the Rooster also includes 1969 (Champagne!), 1993 (red Burgundy), 2005 (great in a lot of places, but red Burgundies are probably too early), and 2017 (great for white Burgundies among other places).

posts.name: Jonathan Grunzweig
posts.cooked:
I have had multiple rewarding encounters of late with the '81 La Mission Haut Brion. Good luck to you!

Jonathan

posts.name: Paul Jaouen
posts.cooked: My favorite vintage ever of Beaucastel. Lots of good Rioja in '81.

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked:
I just did '81s two months ago . Up to $1000 means everything is on the table. I personally would try a couple bottles in a flight or work through courses.

  • William Kelly just had an 81 La Tache and is doing “research” on 81 burgs this year… maybe he has other recommendations if you reach out to him.
  • I enjoyed the 1981 PLL Bordeaux, reasonably priced and beautiful. Not the highest of highs, but solid. Some suggest '81s Bordeaux is generally ready to go.
  • '81 Lopez Heredia Rouge can go another two decades
  • '81 Lopez Heredia Blanco was fun to have next to the Rouge
  • '81 Yquem was great with a savory gorgonzola / pear / endive salad… don’t want crazy strong cheese but medium+
  • '81 Cavallotto San Giuseppe was corked; I had another random '81 last year was punching really well so was bummed to not try a better producer
  • Other threads on WB here have mentioned LMHB, Unico, Beaucastle and maybe ?Montrose?

I think key is to buy whatever bottles without enough time to stand them up for a few weeks/months so the sediment is a non-issue.

posts.name: Peter Z.
posts.cooked: Another vote for Beaucastel and Pichon Comtesse, both excellent.

posts.name: Sean Devaney
posts.cooked: The 1981 Ridge Monte Bello was singing about 10 years ago and seemed to have a lot of life in it.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: There are some lovely Bordeaux, Napa Cabernets, and Champagnes; but the appellation that really excelled in 1981 was Châteauneuf-du-Pape. The brilliant Beaucastel is representative of what was done elsewhere. 1981 Fortia Tête de Cru, for example, was great last month!

posts.name: Paul Roess
posts.cooked:
Wife is '81. Palmer - felt like it was on the downhill slide but decent, LLC - double decanted before restaurant and needed a lot of air still and was gone before it really opened, little green but decent, La Mish - needs air but fantastic, Beaucastel - best Beaucastel I have ever had, Cos - double decanted a magnum before going to restaurant - maybe needed more air, decent.

My rank - Beaucastel, La Mish, LLC, Cos, Palmer
I’ve had good luck with champagnes, especially Dom Ruinart and Bollinger RD.

posts.name: Marshall Manning
posts.cooked:
There are some lovely Bordeaux, Napa Cabernets, and Champagnes; but the appellation that really excelled in 1981 was Châteauneuf-du-Pape. The brilliant Beaucastel is representative of what was done elsewhere. 1981 Fortia Tête de Cru, for example, was great last month!

It’s been a few years, but '81 Vieux Telegraphe was fantastic, too.

posts.name: Nick van der Merwe
posts.cooked:
One idea that we have had fun with is using Zodiac years for birthday wines, which also helps for “off” vintages in a particular year. In your case, Year of the Rooster also includes 1969 (Champagne!), 1993 (red Burgundy), 2005 (great in a lot of places, but red Burgundies are probably too early), and 2017 (great for white Burgundies among other places).

This is a fun idea, thanks!

posts.name: Nick van der Merwe
posts.cooked:
Agree with the above suggestions and would add that 1981 was a strong vintage for classic CA cabernet such as Dunn, Diamond Creek and Ridge, and is one of my favorites. Well stored bottles are well worth seeking out. You can often find large formats, too, which is fun for a party.

Hadn’t thought about magnums, tbh, but that’s a great idea - thank you!

posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.cooked:
1981 Grange is a very good wine and will be in full stride at age 40. Wine Searcher suggests it is available within your price threshold

brodie

Thanks. I tracked that down too - living in Pittsburgh makes a W-S Pro account something of a necessity.

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked: 1981 Pegau has been extraordinary the couple of times I’ve had it, even more than the terrific Beaucastel.

posts.name: Mark Ye
posts.cooked:
• '81 Lopez Heredia Rouge can go another two decades
  • '81 Yquem was great with a savory gorgonzola / pear / endive salad… don’t want crazy strong cheese but medium+

Schweeeet… wife’s birth year and have both of these on deck for July!

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: Beaucastel—another vote for that one n

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: '81 Yquem is very good.

posts.name: Robert Sand
posts.cooked:
Funny that most , not all, writers forget about Chateauneuf-du-Pape … even negoc. bottles can be fine, not only Beaucastel, Pegau , Rayas … many can be found for 100-200 …
Rioja is 2nd choice

posts.name: Robert Sand
posts.cooked:
1981 Pegau has been extraordinary the couple of times I’ve had it, even more than the terrific Beaucastel.

+1

posts.name: Michael Mager
posts.cooked:
• '81 Lopez Heredia Rouge can go another two decades
  • '81 Lopez Heredia Blanco was fun to have next to the Rouge

The '81 Bosconia GR is still probably the best “post-1964” vintage RLdH wine that I’ve tasted. I’d go for that one without hesitation. That being said, the '81 Tondonia Blanco GR is underrated, and (in my opinion) not far behind the Bosconia in quality. Both of those will continue to be in their aging sweet spot for at least another 20 years (and will probably easily outlive me).

Michael

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: Gruaud Larose was particularly good in '81, though I haven’t had one since the 90s.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked: 1981 Lopez de Heredia Bosconia Gran Reserva. Get as much as you can, when you can.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
1981 Pegau has been extraordinary the couple of times I’ve had it, even more than the terrific Beaucastel.

It’s the best Pégaü ever made I venture!

Overall 1981 in Châteauneuf is at least the equal of 1978 in my opinion, yet for whatever reason much less talked about.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Overall 1981 in Châteauneuf is at least the equal of 1978 in my opinion, yet for whatever reason much less talked about.

Perhaps because '81 wasn’t such a great vintage elsewhere, while '78 was very strong in many regions.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked: 81 Beaucastel was fabulous several years ago. LdH was also good. Another good one I didn’t see mentioned was Grange.
I had an '81 Clos des Mouches a few weeks ago that was outstanding.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I had an '81 Clos des Mouches a few weeks ago that was outstanding.

What color and from whom? (Pedantically I might also ask in which commune, as there is also a Clos des Mouches in Meursault)… I’m assuming Drouhin and red, but correct me if I’m wrong!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Gruaud Larose was particularly good in '81, though I haven’t had one since the 90s.

Still tasty!

1981 La Conseillante is a particular favorite, though.

I had an '81 Clos des Mouches a few weeks ago that was outstanding.

What color and from whom? (Pedantically I might also ask in which commune, as there is also a Clos des Mouches in Meursault)… I’m assuming Drouhin and red, but correct me if I’m wrong!

yessir Drouhin, Beaune, and red.

But you’re right I should have specified. In my mind there is just one bottling of Clos des Mouches. But I’m sure if I ever tried the white it would change my mind on that!
